teamObjective.explanation: As the opening leader with a very weak hand (3-6 of spades, no trumps), the immediate team objective is to make a cheap lead that either lets partner win the trick or forces opponents to spend a higher card, setting up future leads.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner has 3 hidden cards and no public signals exist yet. They are likely holding a typical subset of the remaining deck, possibly including some mid-to-high cards or trumps that could win a low lead, but this is highly uncertain.

opponentBelief.summary: Each opponent holds 3 hidden cards. Because no cards have been played, they likely contain some cards higher than my 3-6 and possibly trumps; they will try to win the lead if the price is low enough.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 is the cheapest possible opening. It conserves the slightly higher 4-6 for later tricks, minimizes the value opponents gain if they win, and gives partner the widest range of cards that could beat it.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 6 is my highest non-trump; leading it now uses my best card on a trick I am unlikely to control, leaving only 3-5 and reducing my chance to win a later spade lead.
The 5 is still a middling card; leading it sacrifices more value than the 3 without enough extra chance of winning the trick, since opponents/partner may easily hold higher cards.
The 4 is slightly better than the 3 but still weak; keeping it over the 3 provides marginally more endgame flexibility, while the 3 is the safest probe.

risks: An opponent or partner may be unable or unwilling to beat the 3, letting the trick return to me with no progress in emptying my hand.
An opponent may win with a small non-trump and retain the lead, possibly playing higher combinations I cannot match.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the lowest card I preserve my remaining cards and avoid committing my only strong-ish cards early; this keeps options open for responding to opponent leads.
